page: Amazon Web Services r5b.2xlarge is a memory-optimized instance powered by the AWS Nitro System. It features an x86_64 Intel Xeon 8259CL processor operating at 3.1 GHz, configured with 8 vCPUs across 4 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. The instance is equipped with 64.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, providing a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it offers a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates average multi-core and memory performance alongside weaker single-core capabilities. The instance achieved average ratings in stress-ng multi-core CPU tests (6756.76) and PassMark CPU evaluations (7687.13), while single-core performance in stress-ng (1282.19) and Geekbench (1116.0) fell into the weaker category. Memory bandwidth tests showed average throughput, reaching 376.63 GB/sec for cached small blocks and 44.25 GB/sec for uncached large blocks. Database operations via PassMark were weak at 2586.32, though Redis SET operations maintained an average performance of 6.89 million operations per second.

The r5b.2xlarge presents a clear tradeoff between high memory capacity and moderate compute performance. While its large memory footprint is designed for memory-intensive workloads, its single-threaded processing and compression speeds (such as gzip and bzip3) are relatively weak. Qualitatively, the instance offers resource density suited for workloads requiring significant RAM without demanding top-tier CPU speeds. It is appropriate for memory-bound applications, caching layers like Redis, moderate database operations, and general-purpose enterprise applications that fit within its 64.0 GB memory envelope.
description: Amazon Web Services r5b.2xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 8 vCPUs and 64.0 GB of DDR4 RAM. Powered by an Intel Xeon 8259CL processor running at 3.1 GHz on the Nitro hypervisor, this x86_64 instance provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, and it operates with a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps. Performance benchmarks show average multi-core CPU and memory bandwidth capabilities, contrasted with weaker single-core processing and database operation scores. The instance is suited for memory-intensive workloads, caching layers, and moderate database tasks where memory capacity is prioritized over raw compute speed.
